sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 106, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 105 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 3.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, after moving LEFT 3 times:
- The left agent (with W center, green border) is now at the left edge of the left panel
- The left c-frame is below it
- The right agent (with W center, green border) is still in the connector
So the active agent is the one with W center that was at cols 18-20. Moving LEFT 3 times moved it to cols 15-17 or so (left edge of left panel).
Now I see two agents in the left panel: one at the left edge and one in the connector. The left c-frame is at the bottom.
Wait, looking at the image more carefully:
- Left edge of left panel: agent with W center (green border)
- Below it: left c-frame (dark border) with white interior
- Connector: agent with W center (green border)
So both agents have W centers now? That's strange. Earlier one had c center and one had W center.
Actually, looking at the segmentation from before:
- id=6: N agent at rows 30-32, cols 18-20, W center (id=9)
- id=7: N agent at rows 30-32, cols 27-29, B center (id=10)
So one agent has W center and one has B center. The B center agent is in the connector at cols 27-29.
After moving LEFT 3 times, the active agent (id=6, W center) moved from cols 18-20 to cols 15-17 (left edge of left panel).
Now I need to figure out the goal. Let me think about what configurations might be winning:
1. Both agents inside their respective c-frames
2. Agents matching c-frame colors
3. Agents in specific positions
Given that:
- Left c-frame has c border and w interior
- Right c-frame has c border and w interior
- One agent has W center, one has B center
Maybe the goal is to have:
- W center agent inside a c-frame with w interior (matching W to w)
- B center agent inside a c-frame with ... something else?
But both c-frames have w interiors. So maybe only one agent needs to be inside a c-frame?
Or maybe the goal is different. Let me think about the initial state again.
Initially:
- Left agent: inside left c-frame, c center
- Right agent: outside right c-frame, W center
The left agent was already inside the left c-frame. The right agent was outside the right c-frame. Maybe the goal is to have the right agent inside the right c-frame?
But the right c-frame is in the right panel, and the purple bar blocks movement. So how can the right agent get there?
Wait, maybe the agents can pass through the connector? The connector is a horizontal passage between the panels. If an agent moves RIGHT from the left panel, it enters the connector. If it continues RIGHT, it enters the right panel.
But earlier, when the right agent moved into the connector, the game ended. So maybe moving into the connector is a losing condition?
Unless... the game ended for a different reason. Let me think about what happened:
1. Right agent was at rows 27-29, cols 12-14 (left panel)
2. Moved DOWN 3 times
3. Right agent pushed left c-frame DOWN
4. Left c-frame hit bottom of panel
5. Right agent continued DOWN and RIGHT into connector
6. Game ended
So the game ended when the right agent moved into the connector. But maybe the game ended because the left c-frame was pushed out of position, not because the agent entered the connector.
Actually, looking at the current state, the game is NOT over. The agents are in the connector and the game is still running. So maybe the game ended temporarily but was recovered by clicking.
Let me try a different approach. Let me move the active agent (W center) into the left c-frame and see what happens.
The left c-frame is at rows 32-36, cols 11-15. The active agent is at the left edge of the left panel, around rows 30-32, cols 15-17.
To enter the left c-frame, I need to move the agent DOWN and LEFT.
Wait, looking at the image, the active agent is at the left edge of the left panel, above the left c-frame. The c-frame is below it. So I need to move DOWN to enter the c-frame.
But the c-frame has a w interior, which means it's occupied. Can the agent enter an occupied c-frame?
Actually, looking at the initial state, the left agent was inside the left c-frame, and the c-frame had a w interior. So the agent replaced the w interior. Maybe the agent can enter the c-frame and replace the w interior.
Let me try moving the active agent DOWN into the left c-frame.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("=== CURRENT STATE ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 20:
        boundary = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in boundary]
        cols = [p[1] for p in boundary]
        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, rows[{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols[{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], children={n['children']}")

# Active agent (W center) is at left edge of left panel
# Left c-frame is at rows 32-36, cols 11-15
# Try moving DOWN into the c-frame
action(['DOWN', 'DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
